TextENGNew: It had been one hectic, raucous day.
But once the sun began to sink, the end came quickly.
"Later, Rena and Kei-chan! See you tomorrow!"
"Thanks for today, Mion. It was a great time."
"See you tomorrow!!"
Satoko and Rika-chan headed home too. After parting with Mion, it was just Rena and me, walking home together through the evening air.
"...Thank you for spending the day with me, Keiichi-kun. ...Did you have fun?"
"Yeah. A lot of fun. Feels almost like a waste to go home now!"
"Oh... well then... would you mind if we made a tiny detour...? ...Maybe?"
"A detour? Is it far?"
"It's a bit of a walk, but it won't take long!"
Maybe it was from walking and playing all day — the tiredness had taken some of the edge off me.
With none of my usual impulse to tease, I just nodded quietly.
...We followed a narrow path up a slope, and all at once the view opened wide.
What spread before us looked like a desolate... abandoned construction site.
A mountain of bulky trash had been dumped down the slope toward the stream below.
changeReason: Old says 'garbage...swamp' but JPN has 粗大ゴミ (bulky waste) and 沢 (stream/brook) — wrong type of waste and wrong water feature.

TextENGNew: Illegal dumping, almost certainly. I had a vague memory of it making the newspapers at some point.
"Ufufu! It's been quite a while since I've come here... I wonder what I'll find, I wonder what I'll find!"
"When you say it's been a while... you're here for that... garbage pile?!"
"I-It's not garbage! ...To me, it's a mountain of treasure..."
Rena had already shifted into her trademark kyute mode.
So there was something she considered kyute buried in here?
"...Oh wow... a new pile. ...I can't wait... I can't wait...!"
She picked her way down the unstable slope with effortless hops. ...She really was country-raised.
"Hey, wait up — I'm coming too... Whoops whoa whoa...!"
City kid that I was, I made an absolute mess of it...
"It's fine, just stay right there, Keiichi-kun! It won't take long!"
Rena gestured for me to stop when I tried to follow.
"...Don't fall! Watch your step!"
"I'm fine, I'm fine. This is nothing!"
With little bounding leaps, literally springing from one spot to the next, Rena disappeared behind the mountains of discarded junk.
I didn't like being left behind, but the fatigue from the whole day had caught up with me, and in the end I stayed put.
With the lively Rena gone, a sudden quiet settled over everything around me...
...The song of the higurashi was slowly chilling the air.
A comfortable tiredness was coaxing me toward drowsiness.
"Ah... damn. Sorry, Rena — I just remembered I've got an errand for my mom after this."
"Huh? ...Oh, right..."
Rena looked at me with a mildly disappointed expression.
I fished the crumpled shopping list from the depths of my pocket. ...I feel bad for Rena, but if not now I was going to forget it again.
"She asked me to pick some things up on my way home. ...Which is unfortunate, but..."
"Not at all. But you should hurry then. The shops in Okinomiya close early."
"Sorry about this. See you."
"Sure, see you~."
I parted ways with Rena and started pedaling toward Okinomiya.
When I arrived at the supermarket, the shutters were still up, but the staff were already beginning to close up.
I checked my watch and realized it had gotten late. I hurried inside.
I pulled out my shopping list and started tossing items from the top of the list into my basket — and then, rounding the corner into the produce section, I spotted a familiar back.
"...Huh?... Rika-chan."
It was Rika-chan, standing there with a basket in her hand.
"Mii~☆ Shopping too, Keiichi?"
"Running an errand for my mom. ...Uh, detergent, detergent..."
I kept one eye on the list even while talking to Rika-chan, and with the closing-time music urging me on, I grabbed something that looked right and shoved it in the basket.
"Keiichi. That's not detergent. That's bleach."
"Huh? ...Oh... really?"
I looked at it again. Even the most stubborn tea stains, gone in two minutes...? ...Wait, is bleach different from detergent?
"Very different. Also, you have canned fish in your basket that say 'cats love it' on the side. Do you have a kitty at home, Keiichi?"
Oh... huh? I hadn't even noticed... For the record, there are no animal pets in the Maebara household.
"Nothing good comes from shopping in a rush. Nipah~☆"
...She's not wrong.
When I took a second look at the basket, had I actually put in a single thing from the list? ...What on earth had I been doing?!
To think I was getting grocery shopping lessons from someone so much younger... And looking closely at the list, it even had things written like 'carrots (Ibaraki-grown)'.
...Mom, don't specify the region of origin for vegetables!! It makes absolutely no difference to my stomach whether the carrot grew up in Ibaraki or anywhere else!
"Keiichi. If you had to choose between Australian beef, Chinese beef, American beef, and the finest Ise-Matsusaka beef — which would you want?"
"...I'll go buy those Ibaraki-grown carrots."
Just then, the sudden crunch of gravel and the sense of a presence made me spin around in surprise.
Standing there was a man who looked very much like a photographer, peering at me through his camera.
Tanned, well-built. And yet something about him gave the impression of a man who couldn't quite be relied upon — an oddly vague, indefinite air.
...Well, he didn't look like a bad person.
"Wh-Whoa! ...You startled me!"
The man had been startled by me suddenly turning around, and he made a great show of his surprise.
That was supposed to be my line.
"...I'm the one who's startled here."
"Sorry, sorry. I didn't mean to alarm you. Do you live in Hinamizawa?"
That single question told me he wasn't from the village.
Unbothered by my suspicious look, the man launched into a self-introduction without being asked.
"I'm Tomitake. A freelance photographer. I come to Hinamizawa every so often."
Not that I asked for your name...
"Taking someone's picture without asking them first — is that really considered good manners?"
"Sorry, sorry. My main subjects are wild birds, you see. They've never once turned me down. Ah hahaha!"
So what — he was treating me the same as a bird?
"No, no — a young man lost in thought at dusk was simply too picturesque to resist... I apologize for raising the viewfinder without permission."
...Adults are smooth operators.
He'd buttered me up just right, and my irritation at being startled quietly dissolved.
I had no particular interest in keeping this familiar older man company, and Rena showed no signs of returning.
But Tomitake-san didn't mind my guarded responses at all and just kept talking on his own.
"Keiichi-kun~! Sorry to keep you waiting~! I'm almost done over here!!"
Rena popped her head up from the garbage pile far below on the slope and waved.
"You have company? ...What on earth is she doing down there?"
That's what I'd like to know.
I finished my shopping and walked out of the supermarket side by side with Rika-chan.
Clink, jingle, CLATTER...
Hearing a cascade of small metallic sounds right beside me, I stopped and turned around. Rika-chan must have tripped and dropped her wallet — coins were scattered all across the ground around her.
"Are you alright?... Uh..."
I hurriedly crouched down and helped Rika-chan gather the coins rolling at our feet. She must have had quite a few in there; some had skittered all the way out toward the middle of the driveway.
This might be more trouble than it looks... Just as I was thinking that and scanning the ground around me —
"Oh, Rika-chama. Something the matter?"
"Oh my, Rika-chama. Did some coins fall? Let me help..."
"Hey, can someone check under there too?"
Elderly people who had been walking along the road or chatting nearby all turned at once and, beaming cheerfully, began picking up the scattered coins.
And with the power of numbers, every last coin was retrieved and returned to Rika-chan's hands in no time at all.
"Thank you all so much. Nipah~♪"
"Ohh, such a blessing. Thank you."
changeReason: Old 'Oh, it's nothing. Thank you.' vs new 'Ohh, such a blessing. Thank you.'; JPN もったいない/ありがたや are religious expressions of reverence/gratitude, not casual 'it's nothing.'

TextENGNew: "A blessing, a blessing."
changeReason: Old 'Thank you, thank you' vs new 'A blessing, a blessing'; JPN ありがたや is a religious expression meaning 'how blessed/grateful' used in devotional context, not a simple thank you.

TextENGNew: It should have been us thanking them for picking up the coins, yet the cluster of elderly people had formed a ring around Rika-chan, pressing their palms together in reverence.
"Wh-Wh-What...!!??"

I stood rooted to the spot, utterly bewildered. But the elderly people paid me no mind — every one of them had their hands clasped together, with Rika-chan at the center of the circle, worshipping her with complete devotion.
"What a blessing."
changeReason: Old 'Thank you.' vs new 'What a blessing.'; JPN ありがたや is devotional language reflecting worship of Rika, not casual thanks.

TextENGNew: "What a blessing."
changeReason: Old 'Thank you.' vs new 'What a blessing.'; same devotional context for ありがたや.

TextENGNew: All I could do was stare at the strange scene, dumbfounded.
And then —
changeReason: Old 'And they went.' vs new 'And then --'; JPN ――と、そこへ means 'And then, at that moment' introducing a new arrival, not 'they went.' The old translation is incorrect.

TextENGNew: "Wherever there's a crowd... you can be sure it's Rika."
Startled by the voice, I turned around to find a man standing there who had all the hallmarks of a photographer.
Tanned and solidly built, and yet somehow giving off an unreliable, vaguely indefinite air. He didn't seem like a bad person, but...
"Hey there. Good evening."
I frowned slightly at the man's overly familiar smile.
"Ah... you just thought I was being too familiar, didn't you?"
I jolted.
changeReason: Old translates ギクッ as 'Geez' (annoyance) when it's an onomatopoeia for a startled flinch/jolt — completely different reaction.

TextENGNew: "Hahaha. Looks like I hit the mark."
"N-No, that's..."
Was it showing on my face? ...Anyway, who is this person?
"Ah, I'm Tomitake. A freelance photographer. I come to Hinamizawa every so often to photograph wild birds."
And without further ado, the man introduced himself. I hadn't asked... but I gave him my name in return.
"Um... Tomitake-san. Do you happen to know Rika-chan?"
"Yes, I come to this village often. We've known each other for quite a long time now."
changeReason: Old 'she is very known in village' is garbled/unclear; new 'I come to this village often' correctly translates JPN この村には、よく来るからね (I come to this village often).

TextENGNew: He came to this village often.
From that, I understood that Tomitake-san was not a resident of the village.
"Rika-chan is as popular as ever."
Tomitake-san glanced over at Rika-chan and spoke as though this were a perfectly familiar sight... But still — why did Rika-chan have such a strange hold on the elderly people here?
Perhaps reading my confusion, Tomitake-san turned to face me.
"It looks like you've only recently moved to this village."
"Hm? ...Oh, yes... more or less."
"The bonds between people in Hinamizawa are very strong... Rika-chan has become a kind of spiritual anchor for the people here."
"A spiritual anchor...?"
"Have you heard about what happened during the dam construction project here?"
Just then, Rika-chan finally freed herself from the cluster of elderly people and came toward us.
"...Mii. Sorry to keep you waiting."
Rika-chan was looking a little worn out.
"Well... Maybe she's searching for the corpse from some old murder, I'd guess?"
Tomitake-san's reaction stalled for just a moment.
I'd slipped — I'd answered like I would have around Rena and the others.
"...A grim business, that was. ...They still haven't found it, have they?"
"Hahaha! Sorry to keep you waiting, Keiichi-kun~"
"Did you wait long? ...I guess? ...I guess?"
"I'd better clear out before I get caught in the middle here. And sorry again for startling you, 'Keiichi-kun.'"
Tomitake-san smiled with a meaningful look and slipped away into the fading twilight.
I missed my chance to say something back, which left me feeling oddly out of sorts.
"...Keiichi-kun, are you upset?... Why, I wonder?... I wonder?"
It wasn't Rena's fault at all, but I'd settle for a distraction.
"How did it go? Did you find anything good?"
"Oh, listen, listen! Um... you see! There was... a Randy-kun statue!♪!!"
"A Randy-kun statue?! ...You mean that thing — the life-sized figure they always put out in front of fast food restaurants?"
"Yes! Randy-kun! ...Hau... so kyuuute... I want to take it home... ☆"
I still couldn't quite grasp Rena's definition of kyute, but she clearly had her heart set on it.
"That's garbage, right? You could just take it home if you wanted to."
"It's buried under another pile. I can't dig it out easily... And there's no lights over there, so it gets dark fast..."
Rena was clearly dejected about finding a treasure she couldn't bring back.
"I'll help you. Consider it repayment for that delicious lunch today."
"...Hau... th-thank you..."
Birds heading home for the night announced that darkness would soon be upon us.
"Keiichi-kun is going to help... I can take Randy-kun home... hau..."
Rena walked along in a dreamy, floaty daze, her steps unsteady as if she were tiptoeing on clouds.
I tried to ask her casually, so as not to disturb her happy mood.
"...Hey, Rena. Did something happen there, a long time ago?"
"Apparently they were building a dam there. I don't know the details, though... hau..."
"Like, something that happened during the construction. An accident, maybe."
"I don't know."
The answer was unusually clear.
It had the ring not so much of an answer as a refusal.
I must have looked quite bewildered. Rena quickly softened her expression.
"Actually, I lived somewhere else until last year."
"Huh?... You transferred here too?... I had just assumed..."
"So, you see, I don't really know much about things before that... Sorry.☆"
I don't know — and I don't want to talk about it. That was the feeling I got.
When I thought about it, of course that made sense. This wasn't a topic that could possibly be pleasant for a girl.
(...A grim business, that was. ...They still haven't found it, have they?)
If what Tomitake-san said was accurate...
Then perhaps only the higurashi knew the truth.
"She looks like she's on a treasure hunt, honestly... even here."
"A treasure hunt?"
Tomitake-san looked more puzzled by the moment. Can't blame him... I'd kind of like to know myself what anyone could find worth having in a dump like this.
Rena was scrambling up the slope toward us with all her might. Tomitake-san watched her for a while, then turned to me with a quiet smile.
"Well, good luck. See you."
And with those words, he faded away into the twilight.
Good luck... with what, exactly???
"Ahaha! Keiichi-kun, thanks for waiting~! Did you have to wait long? ...Long?"
Rena came back wearing a wide, radiant smile.
Wait — did he mean good luck... with Rena?! Did that guy just casually walk off with one massive misunderstanding under his belt?!
"Oh, Keiichi-kun... what's the matter?... Your face looks a bit..."
"Huh?! No, no — it's not red! My face is definitely not red!"
I'd let my imagination get the better of me.
"So, how did it go? Find anything worth digging out?"
"Oh yes, listen, listen! Um... you see! There was a Randy-kun statue!♪!!"
"A Randy-kun statue? ...Is that the thing — the life-sized figure they put out in front of fast food places?"
"Yes, Randy-kun! ...Hau, so kyuuute~~. I want to take it home~~♪"
...I had absolutely no idea what criteria defined kyute or how anything qualified, but since Rena clearly had her heart set on it, I'd just go with that.
"That's garbage, right? No one would care if you just took it home."
"It's buried under another pile. I can't dig it out easily... And there's no lights over there, so it gets dark fast..."
Rena was clearly crestfallen about finding a treasure she couldn't take back.
"I'll help you dig it out. Consider it my way of repaying you for today's delicious lunch."
"Really?! ...Hau... th-thank you..."
Rena began walking in a dreamy, floaty daze, her steps unsteady as if she were drifting through clouds.
"Rena?"
"Keiichi-kun is going to help... I can take Randy-kun home... hau..."
I genuinely had no idea what standard of judgment made a Randy-kun statue a worthy find... but, well... I'd let it go.
To the cicadas' chorus, I hummed softly to myself as I walked home behind Rena.
"Rika-chan is just so adorable."
I turned to Rika-chan as I said it and smiled. She tilted her head with a little "mii?" and was smoothing down her dark hair, which had been disheveled from all the jostling.
"...What are you talking about, Keiichi?"
"Oh, I was just thinking — I think I understand why everyone wants to fuss over Rika-chan."
Take Rena for example, and Rena, and... also Rena. If she were here right now, she'd probably flatten the crowd of elderly people saying her usual 'I'm taking you home~♪' and— well, that wouldn't do.
"...I'm glad to hear you say that. But I'd appreciate it if you didn't rub my hair quite so hard — you've made a mess of it."
Rika-chan said that with a little pout. Seeing that adorable gesture, both Tomitake-san and I burst out laughing.
"Satoko-chan is just as cute as Rika-chan, you know..."
"What do you mean by that?"
"...Tomitake, you were here?"
"It's been a while, Rika-chan."
"It has. Were you able to take some good pictures?"
"Ah hahaha! Thanks to this place, I've found all sorts of interesting things again."
"I'm glad to hear that. Nipah~☆"
Rika-chan returned the smile without a hint of reserve. It really did seem they knew each other.
"Tomitake is a photographer. Though, if you'll pardon me saying so, his skills are nothing special."
"Haha... You're harsh, Rika-chan."
Tomitake-san chuckled and scratched his head.
"What brings you to a place like this, Tomitake?"
"I just stopped by to pick up a few things. But what about you, Rika-chan?"
"I'm shopping for dinner. It's my turn today. Nipah~"
The shopping bag hanging from Rika-chan's arm had green onions poking out of the top. If it was her turn, did that mean Rika-chan would be cooking dinner tonight?
"I'd love to try Rika-chan's cooking one of these days."
"Next time I'll treat Tomitake to a fugu hot pot. Thrills included — there's always a chance of a winning number."
"A winning number in a dish..."
"How about you, Keiichi?"
I'll pass at full force. ...That said — watching her say something so genuinely dangerous, the word "poison" right there in plain sight, while never losing that adorable smile was just...
No, no, no! This must be Rena's influence!! ...I was starting to worry I was losing myself.
"Ah hahaha! Rika-chan always wins in the end. Well, I have somewhere to be — I'll leave you two to it."
And with that, Tomitake-san disappeared off into the darkening town.
"I suppose I'll head home too."
"Ah, Keiichi — I want to give you one of these."
As she said that, Rika-chan held out a giant imagawayaki — or rather, something so large you could almost call it a tray-yaki.
"My share, for you."
"My... share?"
"When I bought things earlier, the shopkeeper gave me some as a freebie. There's still this many left."
"Hold on — all of this?!"
I stared wide-eyed at the paper bag stuffed full of imagawayaki at Rika-chan's feet. Was this, too, the result of her extraordinary popularity?
Rika-chan's popularity was perhaps even greater than I had realized...
"Oh, thanks. Well then — itadakimasu~"
I opened my mouth wide and took a big bite. The sweet taste of warm bean paste spread all through my mouth.
...Yeah, delicious! The dough around it was pleasantly soft and mellow too.
"So, with that said — Keiichi."
"Hm?"
"My hands are quite full just with this shopping bag, you see."
"So, with that said — Keiichi."
"...Understood."
"Nipah~~☆"
You sly little... negotiating after I've already eaten...! Argh!
And so, that is how things came to be.
I ended up carrying bags for twice as long and with twice the effort as originally planned...
"It's charisma, if you ask me."
"Charisma?... What does that mean?"
Rika-chan tilted her head with a small "mii..." And that gesture was just so unbearably cute that I felt a pang in my chest and very nearly wanted to take her home — wait, WHAT!
This has to be Rena's influence!!
I cleared my throat to shake the wayward thought loose and spoke.
"Well... Charisma is... the power to draw people in, I guess? Like an innate mystique..."
"Mystique?... Am I one of Cao Cao's generals from the Three Kingdoms?"
"That's not the direction I'm familiar with for that joke."
"Miii?"
Rika-chan kept her head tilted in a blank sort of way. Well, since I'd brought it up, I pressed on.
"Hmm... I suppose it means you have a quality that naturally draws people toward you."
"...I don't quite understand what you're trying to say, Keiichi."

"For example — imagine you're walking through a dense, dark forest, and you get the feeling that something might be there... that something might come out. It's frightening, but you want to see what it is. Like — if it's going to show itself, show itself."
"Yeah. I know that feeling. Hinamizawa itself has that kind of pull."
"Humans get very sensitive about things they can't understand."
"You feel that it's beyond your own power, and you find yourself drawn to it. That's what I mean by mystique... And a person who has that kind of pull is what you'd call charismatic."
I finished the thought and studied Rika-chan's face.
"...So I'm charismatic? ...I'm not sure I fully understand, but it sounds a little interesting. Nipah~☆"
Rika-chan smiled contentedly as she said it. ...Whether she'd grasped the precise meaning was doubtful, but she seemed satisfied for now, which was a relief.
"Haha... It's said that Rika-chan is a reincarnation of Oyashiro-sama."
"...Oyashiro-sama?"
I leaned forward to ask what that meant — and a pleasantly fragrant object was nudged right up to my nose.
"My share, for Keiichi too. The shopkeeper gave me this as a freebie earlier."
Rika-chan smiled and placed it in my hands. Still warm and steaming, it was a large imagawayaki — so large you might almost call it a platter-yaki.
"Here. For Tomitake too, if you'd like."
"Hm?... Oh, no thank you. I'm not really one for sweets."
"Tomitake-san... about that 'Oyashiro-sama'—"
"—Tomitake, they're closing soon."
Rika-chan said that and pointed to the shop behind her. And indeed, just as she said, a wagon of goods that had been set out near the entrance was being wheeled back inside.
"Oh! Of course, I almost forgot. —Well then, you two, see you."
With that and a wave of his hand, Tomitake-san walked into the shop.
A vaguely anticlimactic, flat feeling. ...When I looked back at Rika-chan, she was happily pulling an imagawayaki out of her paper bag and taking a modest bite with her small mouth.
"Hey... Rika-chan. Who is Oyashiro-sama?"
"Miii~. I'm not entirely sure. But I'm perfectly happy with imagawayaki. Nipah~☆"
With that, Rika-chan polished off an imagawayaki and tucked her shopping bag and the bag of pastries into her bicycle basket.
Her face held a smile, but something in the air made me feel I couldn't ask any further.
"...Well then, Rika-chan. See you tomorrow at school."
With that, I climbed onto my bike and started pedaling.
...After I'd gone a little way and glanced back, Rika-chan was still standing there by her bicycle, not having moved.
Her face was hidden by the dim, fading light of evening, and I couldn't quite... make it out.
